request.question: Who played bass for the debut album of the band that dissolved in 2011, and had a lead singer what born in 1960

title: Gordon Copley
Regina Zernay Roberts
The Crackerjacks (band)
Michael Stipe
Ivy Jenkins
Mike Davenport
Murmur (album)
No Gods
Joe Raposo (bassist)
Steve Pfauter
sentences: Gordon Copley, born in Pasadena, California, is a bassist who briefly played bass for Black Sabbath.
He played bass on the Black Sabbath song "No Stranger to Love" on their album "Seventh Star".
Bassist Dave Spitz played bass on the rest of the songs on the album.
Copley was also the bassist for Lita Ford's band in early 1985 and joined her on tour after featuring on Seventh Star, leaving Sabbath.
Copley would then later feature on Bill Ward's 1990 album .
Regina Zernay Roberts is an American instrumentalist, singer, and songwriter.
She is lead singer and bass player of Detroit Diesel Power.
She played bass guitar and Moog synthesizer with Scarlet Fever, the all-female backing band that toured with Cee Lo Green during 2010 and 2011.
She toured several years in the New Orleans cowpunk band, Cowboy Mouth; was founding bassist/singer of Los Angeles punk rock band, Méchant; and was bassist for the hard rock band, Halfcocked.
The Crackerjacks were a 1960s Memphis garage rock group.
Band members included organist-bassist Bobby Sowell, lead guitarist David Preola, lead singer Jerry Stamson, and drummer Roy Yeager.
They gained popularity in 1966–67, regularly appearing on Memphis WHBQ TV Talent Party with George Klien (also a popular radio DJ and friend of Elvis Presley).
They won the Mid-South Fair in 1966 for best group.
The Crackerjacks had no bass player; Bobby Sowell played bass and organ at the same time.
The group was short lived due to several reasons.
Sowell was drafted in 1968, Preola also was drafted into the Army, Stampson went into the restaurant business and Yeager went with the group Lobo and the southern rock band Atlanta Rhythm Section.
Sowell was inducted into the Rockabilly Hall of Fame in 2002.
The Crackerjacks are featured in a rock book, "History of Memphis Bands, 1960-72" by Ron Hall, available at bookstores.
John Michael Stipe (born January 4, 1960) is an American singer, songwriter, musician, film producer, music video director, visual artist, and philanthropist.
He is best known as the lead singer of the alternative rock band R.E.M. from their formation in 1980 until their dissolution in 2011.
Ivana Jenkins (née) Vujić (Serbian: Ивана Вујић , born June 15, 1983), known by her stage name Ivy Jenkins is a Serbian-born Canadian bassist.
She played bass for the metal, all-woman band Kittie from October 2007 until March 2012.
She began her career with Kittie by initially filling in for ailing bassist Trish Doan in late 2007, a period which found the band playing concerts in Central and South America.
Before she was in Kittie, Ivy played bass for the melodic hard rock band In The Wake from Toronto, Canada.
In 2008, it was officially announced that Ivy was a full-time member of Kittie and in 2009 she appeared on their fifth studio CD, "In The Black".
Ivy also wrote and recorded bass for Kittie's sixth album, "I've Failed" "You", which was released on August 15, 2011.
Jenkins plays a black Warwick Corvette 5 string bass guitar.
Michael Sean Davenport (born 1968 in Orcutt, California) is an American musician.
Davenport was bass guitarist for The Ataris, joining the band in 1995 prior to the album "Blue Skies, Broken Hearts...Next 12 Exits" in which Davenport co-wrote the song "In Spite of the World".
He also played bass on the album "End Is Forever" and the EP "Look Forward to Failure" on Fat Wreck Chords.
After numerous recordings, tours, and a major label release with The Ataris, "So Long Astoria" in which he co-wrote the song "The Hero Dies in this One" Davenport played bass on "Live at the Metro".
In 2004 Davenport left the band after the Ataris headed in a different musical direction.
In 2005 Davenport appeared in the documentary movie Punks Not Dead www.punksnotdeadthemovie.com "Punk's Not Dead."
Murmur is the debut studio album by the American alternative rock band R.E.M., released on April 12, 1983 by I.R.S. Records.

"Murmur" drew critical acclaim upon its release for its unusual sound, defined by lead singer Michael Stipe's cryptic lyrics, guitarist Peter Buck's jangly guitar style, and bass guitarist Mike Mills' melodic basslines.
No Gods is the debut full-length studio album by British rock band Sharks which was released by Rise Records on 19 March 2012 in the UK and on 20 March 2012 in the US.
The album was recorded in Baltimore, MD, from September to early October 2011 and was produced by Brian McTernan.
The band recorded a total of 14 tracks.
Tony Corrales, formerly of The Exposed, having joined Sharks on a permanent basis, played bass on three songs (James Mattock played bass on all other tracks).
Joe Raposo (born 1970), of Portuguese descent, is the bassist for the seminal California punk rock band Lagwagon and qa engineer lead at Zynga.
He also played bass for a period of time for The Real Mckenzies and Mad Caddies.
Additionally, Raposo plays bass for the San Francisco-based fusion group King City, with fellow Lagwagon member Chris Rest as well as filling in on bass for several live shows with the Dwarves.
Raposo began his career in 1987 at the age of seventeen by joining California hardcore punk band Rich Kids on LSD as their new bassist and remained with RKL until their hiatus in 1996.
He began playing shows with RKL again in 2003 (after they had re-formed a year prior) until their current hiatus after the death of lead singer Jason Sears.
Raposo joined Lagwagon in 2010, replacing original bassist Jesse Buglione.
Raposo also plays bass in the cover band Uke-Hunt.
Steve Pfauter ("Humann") (born 1963, in Francisco, California) is a musician and set dresser and former Bass Guitarist for the Punk rock band The Vandals, from 1980-1984.

Steve was born to German immigrants and attended Los Alamitos High, he joined The Vandals in 1980 and played bass guitar on their debut EP, "Peace thru Vandalism" Steve Human (A.K.A. Human T-Shirt, Humann) designed T-Shirts for T.S.O.L. and also did art work for the Dead Kennedys.
Steve has also played bass in the Original Los Angeles punk rock band Detox, making two full-length albums which featured six songs he wrote while touring the US and Canada.
Later he joined Ken All Night Rocker.
Human also roadied for T.S.O.L. on their first two nationwide tours in 1980-1982.
He was once known for T-shirt designs owning his own silkscreen shop at age sixteen.
He now works in the movie industry as a set dresser and worked on productions such as "Leaving Las Vegas", "High Crimes" and "Spider-Man 3".

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

You are the first-hop summarizer. Compress the supplied passages into a short, actionable intermediate state that lets the next agent know exactly what is present in the passages, what is missing, and what to fetch next. Produce a single `summary` string only.

Summary content requirements (concise: a few short sentences or clauses):
- First-hop evidence: one short paraphrase or a very brief quoted phrase from the passages that links the relevant entities or facts supporting any partial answer. Do not copy long verbatim spans.
- Bridge entity: explicitly name the person/entity mentioned in the passages that the second hop should investigate (use the full name or label exactly as it appears in the passages). If the question uses only a given name and the passages do not disambiguate which individual is meant, state clearly that the identity is missing/ambiguous.
- Missing fact: state in one short sentence which specific fact is required to answer the question (for example: winner name, year, birthplace, or another identifying detail) and confirm it is not present in the given passages.
- Retrieval directions: give one concise, ordered, actionable next-hop plan (1–2 steps). Prefer concrete target pages or query patterns, e.g. "open the named person's biography to check for [missing fact]" or a short query pattern such as '"<NAME>" birthplace OR "born in" site:wikipedia.org' or 'open a chronological list of <AWARD> winners and search for <NAME>'. Do not instruct the system to execute retrieval here — only specify what the next retrieval should fetch.
- Clarification: if ambiguity persists (e.g., only a given name is provided and multiple candidates are possible), include a short clarifying request asking the user to provide the full name.

Hard constraints and tone:
- Be concise and factual. Keep the same neutral, summary tone as before.
- Do not guess, infer, or assert any facts not present in the passages (no candidate answers, no years, no birthplace assertions, etc.).
- Do not ask the user for permission to search external sources. Give explicit next-hop retrieval directions instead.
- Output only the single `summary` field text complying with the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict: Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

You are integrating newly retrieved passages (second‑hop evidence) with the first‑hop context to produce a grounded, post‑retrieval summary that preserves the facts the final answer generator will need. Produce a single field `summary` whose value is 1–3 concise sentences that: (1) directly state the answer to the question (e.g., where the studios/recordings were located) based on the available evidence; (2) if any passage explicitly names a production facility or studio and ties that facility to a specific locality (for example by giving the facility name together with a street, town, or county), include one short supporting sentence that paraphrases that passage to show the link between the facility and the locality; and (3) include one very short sentence or clause that synthesizes the context and the supporting passage to place the studios in the stated locality. Paraphrase supporting passages; do not include long verbatim quotations.

If the passages do not conclusively link a named studio to a specific locality, preserve and state that uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported specifics. Avoid headers, meta commentary, long quotations, extra sections, or inventing details not present in the provided context/passages. Keep phrasing compact, factual, and focused on the evidence needed to support the final answer.

Output contract: produce only the `summary` field (1–3 concise sentences) that integrates the first‑hop context with the second‑hop passages as described above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer` as follows. Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) as the evidential basis for the final answer; do not add external facts except where the instructions below explicitly allow a limited, well‑justified use of internal background knowledge. Always produce the final result inside the [[ ## answer ## ]] block and make that block contain only the minimal answer string required by these rules.

1) General minimal-answer rule
- If the summaries unambiguously support a single short answer (a short name, title, demonym, year, or single noun/phrase of roughly 1–5 words), emit only that minimal token sequence as the answer. Normalize the chosen substring by removing any leading/trailing whitespace or surrounding punctuation but preserve the phrase's internal characters and capitalization exactly as shown in the summaries. Do not add quotes, em dashes, braces, labels, trailing punctuation, explanatory clauses, or any additional text.

2) When both summaries agree on an identical short phrase
- If summary_1 and summary_2 contain the same short named-entity string (after lowercasing only for agreement detection), extract that common phrase, strip surrounding punctuation/whitespace, and emit it verbatim (preserving original capitalization) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]] with no surrounding or appended characters.

3) Patterned question triggers (minimal formatting + deterministic selection)
- 'Which ... is from X, A or B?': When the question asks which of two named options is from a place and the summaries clearly indicate a single band/person as the answer, emit only the minimal band/person name supported by the summaries.
- Comparative membership questions (e.g., 'Does A or B have more band members?'): If the summaries clearly indicate which band has more members, emit only that band's name.
- Short common-name questions (e.g., 'What do people in the UK commonly call the location ...?'): If both summaries provide the same short common name, emit only that short name string.

4) Nationality questions
- If the question asks for a nationality/demonym and either summary_1 or summary_2 contains a demonym, emit only the minimal demonym token/phrase. Prefer the first demonym found in summary_1; if none appears in summary_1, use the first found in summary_2. Emit it verbatim (no added words or punctuation).

5) Two-performer composition specificity
- If the question asks for the "type of musical composition" used by a named act and both summaries use a generic word like 'song(s)', you may consult internal knowledge only to determine whether the act is a two-person (duo) group. If you can reliably confirm the act is a two-person group, replace the generic term with the single-word or very short noun that names a composition intended for two performers (for example, 'duet') and emit that single word as the minimal answer. If you cannot reliably confirm duo membership, emit the minimal noun exactly as it appears in the summaries.

6) Narrow assumed referent (single-name trigger)
- If the question supplies only a given name (e.g., 'Maurice') and the visible summaries explicitly mention a specific example with that given name (e.g., a named example appears in the summaries) and the summaries identify the requested fact (e.g., the award), then you may assume that referent and emit the minimal answer (for example, a single year) if you have sufficient internal confidence. If you do not have sufficient internal confidence, follow the normal clarification behavior (emit a brief clarifying question as the answer field) rather than guessing.

7) What to avoid (formatting and content prohibitions)
- Never add explanatory text, full sentences, comparisons, justifications, or provenance in the answer field. Do not append decorative punctuation (em dashes, extra commas, trailing periods), quotes, braces, JSON-like structures, leading newlines, or any characters not part of the normalized minimal phrase. Do not invent facts not present in the summaries except where the two narrowly scoped rules above permit limited internal confirmation (duo-confirmation and the single-name assumed referent). If the summaries do not unambiguously support a single minimal answer and you lack permitted internal justification, emit a concise clarification request as the answer.

Additional specific, evidence-directed selection rules (apply these only when their trigger conditions are met, otherwise follow the rules above):

8) Prefer an explicitly named immediate jurisdiction when resolving county/country wording conflicts
- Trigger: The question asks which country/sovereign-state a county or borough is part of (phrasing like 'county' and 'part of what country' or equivalent) and the summaries disagree or one summary uses a parenthetical membership note such as 'A (part of B)'.
- Behavior: If one summary contains a parenthetical of the form 'A (part of B)', treat the pre-parenthetical name A as the immediate administrative region referenced by the summaries and emit A (trimmed of surrounding punctuation/whitespace) as the minimal answer. More generally, when summaries disagree about a place, prefer the summary that directly names the immediate jurisdiction associated with the county (the subnational or constituent area named in the summary) and extract that short named phrase verbatim as the answer. Do not add any extra words or punctuation.
- Fallback: If neither summary clearly names an immediate jurisdiction or both are equally ambiguous, emit a concise clarification question per the base rules rather than guessing.

9) Size-answer simplification when both summaries agree with an inclusive qualifier
- Trigger: The question explicitly asks for a size (phrasing like 'what size' or 'equal to or smaller than what size' or equivalent) AND summary_1 and summary_2 are identical strings for agreement-detection (case-insensitive) AND that common string matches the pattern '<size> or smaller' or '<size> or less' (case-insensitive, allowing optional surrounding punctuation/whitespace).
- Behavior: Produce as the minimal answer only the '<size>' portion of the agreed common phrase by removing the trailing inclusive qualifier 'or smaller' or 'or less' and any immediately adjacent punctuation/extra whitespace. Emit the remaining size token (number plus unit) verbatim as shown in the summaries (preserve digits, unit spelling/capitalization, and internal spacing).
- Do not apply this edit when the summaries do not exactly agree, when the common phrase does not match the pattern, or when the question is not asking for a size.

10) Prefer identical parenthetical short-names when present in both summaries
- Trigger: Both summary_1 and summary_2 each contain an identical parenthetical short-name substring (a substring of the form '(' ... ')' that, once trimmed, is 1–5 words long, agreement checked case-insensitively).
- Behavior: Extract the parenthetical content (remove the surrounding '(' and ')', trim surrounding whitespace and surrounding punctuation) and treat that extracted short-name as the canonical minimal answer. Emit it verbatim (preserving internal characters and capitalization exactly as shown in the summaries) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]]. Do not emit the longer formal name when such an identical parenthetical is present in both summaries.

Output contract reminder: The produced `answer` must be exactly the minimal answer string determined by the rules above (or a short clarification question when required). The [[ ## answer ## ]] block must contain only that string and nothing else.

response.hop1_query: Who played bass for the debut album of the band that dissolved in 2011, and had a lead singer what born in 1960
hop1_docs: Islands (Kajagoogoo album) | Islands is the second album by the British pop band Kajagoogoo, released on 21 May 1984 on the EMI label.  This was the band's first album without lead vocalist Limahl; who had been fired by the band in mid-1983 and went on to pursue a solo career.  Bass player Nick Beggs, already the group's main backing singer, took over lead vocal duties, and also wrote the lyrics.  The album was co-produced by the band themselves, now a four-piece group, along with Colin Thurston, who had also produced their debut, "White Feathers".  It is the final album to-date to feature founding drummer Jez Strode.

Rockaholic | Rockaholic is the eighth studio album by American rock band Warrant, released on May 17, 2011.  The album features the band's third lead singer, Robert Mason, who replaced original lead singer Jani Lane in 2008.  Lane returned to the band for a 2008 reunion tour but by the end of the year, he and the band parted ways for the second time.  Lane had previously replaced former lead singer Jaime St. James, who performed lead vocals on the band's last album, "Born Again", and who initially replaced Lane in 2004.

Fuze (band) | Fuze are an English pop rock band who formed in 2010.  The band consists of Ed Alston (lead vocals and guitar), George Kirchner (lead guitar and vocals), Jack Goldsmith (bass guitar and backing vocals) and Keir Adamson (drums and backing vocals).  They were signed by LUMI Records in 2011 prior to the release of their debut album, "That's What She Said".  The band's name originates from the American drink, Fuze.  Their debut single, Hey You, was used in the soundtrack for the UK film, The Shouting Men, which featured Craig Fairbrass, Warren Llambias, John Barnes and Matt Daniel-Baker.  Their second single, When You Come Home, was released in July 2011.  Fuze were crowned Chelmsford's YFest winners in August 2011 after return from their tour of England.  The band are believed to have finished writing for their second album, which is due for release in 2012.

Andalucia (album) | Andalucia is the fourth studio album by Los Angeles rock band Tito & Tarantula, released in 2002.  The album marked several line-up changes in the band, which had previously consisted of lead singer/rhythm guitarist Tito Larriva, lead guitarist Peter Atanasoff, and drummer Johnny "Vatos" Hernandez.  This album featured the debut of lead guitarist Steven Hufsteter – who had previously played with Larriva in the Cruzados, bassist Io Perry, and keyboardist Marcus Praed.

This Is What Rock n' Roll Looks Like | "This Is What Rock n' Roll Looks Like" is the debut single of American industrial singer-songwriter Porcelain Black, featuring American rapper Lil Wayne.  This song serves as the lead single from the singer's debut studio album, "Mannequin Factory".  Produced by Moroccan-Swedish producer RedOne, who had originally discovered Black, "This Is What Rock n' Roll Looks Like" is influenced by the genre of rock, lyrically stating how the listener should be proud of who they are.  The song was released digitally in the United States on March 29, 2011, and managed to chart at number forty-four on the US "Billboard" Hot Dance Club Songs.

Okie Baroque | Okie Baroque is an EP by American indie rock band Blackpool Lights.  After touring to support their 2006 debut album This Town's Disaster, the band dissolved in 2008 for unknown reasons.  In early 2010 after the reunion of lead singer Jim Suptic's other band The Get Up Kids, it was announced on Twitter that Blackpool Lights had reunited and was recording new material.  The album was released online on November 30, 2010.

Rick Savage | Richard "Rick" Savage (born 2 December 1960) is the bass guitarist and one of the founding members of the English rock band, Def Leppard.  He is called "Sav" to avoid confusion between him and the other Rick in the band, drummer Rick Allen.  Savage and lead singer Joe Elliott are the only remaining original members of the band.  The two of them, plus Allen, are also the only members of the band who have performed on every album.
hop1_titles: This Is What Rock n' Roll Looks Like
response.summary_1: First-hop evidence: passage [7] states "Richard 'Rick' Savage (born 2 December 1960) is the bass guitarist ... Def Leppard." Bridge entity: the passages mention Blackpool Lights and Richard "Rick" Savage, but no band is described as "dissolved in 2011" (Blackpool Lights dissolved in 2008). Missing fact: the identity of the band that dissolved in 2011 (and its lead singer born in 1960) and the name of the bassist on that band's debut album are not present. Retrieval directions: (1) open "List of bands disestablished in 2011" (or run search '"disbanded" OR "dissolved" 2011 site:wikipedia.org') to identify candidate bands and open each band's Wikipedia page to check the lead singer's birth year; (2) once the correct band is identified, open that band's debut-album page or the band's discography/album personnel section to read the credited bass player. Clarification: if you know the band's name, please provide it (the passages do not identify a band dissolved in 2011).
response.hop2_query:
1) Open: "List of bands disestablished in 2011 - Wikipedia" (or search site:wikipedia.org "bands disestablished in 2011" / "bands disbanded in 2011").  
2) From that list, open each band's Wikipedia page and check the lead singer's birth year; identify the band whose lead singer was born in 1960. (If multiple, return all matches.)  
3) Once the correct band is identified, open that band's page and then the page for their debut album (or the band's discography / album personnel section) and extract the name of the musician credited as bass (bass guitar) on the debut album. Provide the band name, the lead singer (with birth year), and the bassist credited on the debut album, with citations (the specific Wikipedia pages/sections used). 

If unable to find on Wikipedia, broaden search to reliable sources (music databases, official band sites, AllMusic, Discogs) for the debut-album personnel.
hop2_docs: Sincerely (Dwight Twilley Band album) | Sincerely is the debut album from the Dwight Twilley Band, released in 1976 on Shelter Records.  The band consisted solely of Dwight Twilley (guitar, piano, lead and harmony vocals) and Phil Seymour (drums, bass, percussion, lead and harmony vocals), although touring band member Bill Pitcock IV played lead guitar on every song except for "Sincerely" itself.  The album credited production to Oister, which was the original name of the Dwight Twilley Band; later reissues have credited production directly to Twilley and Seymour.

List of pipe bands | A pipe band is a musical ensemble consisting of pipers and drummers.  There are many such bands in the world, which play for ceremonial purposes, recreation, competition or all three.  This list encompasses only notable pipe bands with their own Wikipedia page.

Fuze (band) | Fuze are an English pop rock band who formed in 2010.  The band consists of Ed Alston (lead vocals and guitar), George Kirchner (lead guitar and vocals), Jack Goldsmith (bass guitar and backing vocals) and Keir Adamson (drums and backing vocals).  They were signed by LUMI Records in 2011 prior to the release of their debut album, "That's What She Said".  The band's name originates from the American drink, Fuze.  Their debut single, Hey You, was used in the soundtrack for the UK film, The Shouting Men, which featured Craig Fairbrass, Warren Llambias, John Barnes and Matt Daniel-Baker.  Their second single, When You Come Home, was released in July 2011.  Fuze were crowned Chelmsford's YFest winners in August 2011 after return from their tour of England.  The band are believed to have finished writing for their second album, which is due for release in 2012.
List of bands from British Columbia | This is a list of bands originating from the Canadian province of British Columbia.  Only bands with valid Wikipedia pages (and thereby notable) are listed.

Speak (band) | Speak (stylized SPEAK) is a synthpop band from Austin, Texas formed in 2008.  The band consists of Troupe Gammage (keyboard and lead vocals), Nick Hurt (guitar and vocals), Joey Delahoussaye (bass and vocals), and Jake Stewart (drums).  The band released their debut single "Carrie" in 2011, and their debut album I Believe In Everything in late 2011.

Glen Check (band) | Glen Check is a South Korean indie band, consisting of singer and guitar player June-One Kim and bass and synthesizer player Hyuk-Jun Kang.  They debuted in 2011 with the release of "Disco Elevator EP" and were joined by drummer Jeon-Yeol Ryu for their first full album, "Haute Couture", which was released in early 2012.  Later that year, Ryu left the group.  Once again a duo, Glen Check released the five-track EP "Cliché" later that year.  In November 2013, a sophomore album was released, called "Youth! " The band has also released several remix EPs.

Mr. Mister | Mr. Mister was an American pop rock band most popular in the 1980s.  The band's name came from an inside joke about a Weather Report album called "Mr. Gone" where they referred to each other as "Mister This" or "Mister That", and eventually selected "Mr. Mister".  The band consisted of Richard Page on lead vocals and bass guitar, Steve George on keyboards/backing vocals, Pat Mastelotto on acoustic and electronic drums/percussion and Steve Farris on guitars/backing vocals.  Mr. Mister was the successor to the band Pages, fronted by Page and George from 1978 to 1981.
